title: Cat Swollen Nail Bed with Pus: Causes and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's nail bed swollen with pus? Learn about Paronychia, urgency levels, and why a photo is vital for your vet to assess the infection.
content_html:
What is a Swollen Nail Bed with Pus?

When a cat has a swollen, painful nail bed accompanied by pus, it is typically a condition known as paronychia. This is a localized infection of the soft tissue surrounding the claw. It often occurs after a traumatic injury, such as a broken nail, a bite wound, or an overgrown claw that has pierced the paw pad. The presence of pus indicates that the body is fighting a bacterial or fungal infection that has taken hold in the sensitive tissues of the nail fold.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While a nail bed infection is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is significantly painful for your cat and requires medical intervention. You should aim to have your cat seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. Delaying treatment can allow the infection to spread to the underlying bone (osteomyelitis) or lead to systemic illness. If you notice your cat is lethargic, hiding, or refusing to eat, seek veterinary care more urgently.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of the affected paw can be a vital tool for triage. A visual assessment allows a veterinary professional to see the degree of swelling, the color and consistency of the discharge, and whether the nail itself is still intact or fractured. This information helps determine the severity of the infection and allows the clinic to prioritize your cat’s appointment appropriately or provide specific first-aid instructions, such as using an Elizabethan collar to prevent licking, until you can arrive.

faqs.question: Can I drain the pus or pop the swelling at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to squeeze or drain a nail bed infection at home. This is extremely painful for the cat and can push the bacteria deeper into the toe, potentially causing a bone infection.

faqs.question: Can I use human antibiotic ointment on my cat's paw?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid human ointments like Neosporin unless directed by a vet, as many contain ingredients that are toxic if licked off. The most important step is to prevent the cat from licking the area until a vet can prescribe safe medication.

faqs.question: What will the veterinarian do for a nail bed infection?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely perform a physical exam, clean the area (sometimes under sedation if it is too painful), and prescribe a course of antibiotics. They may also take a small sample of the pus to identify the specific bacteria involved.
